coredata.dc:title: Facile and green approach to prepare fluorescent carbon dots: Emergent nanomaterial for cell imaging and detection of vitamin B2
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Kundu, Aniruddha
Nandi, Sudipta
Das, Pradip
Nandi, Arun K.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are a new representative in carbonaceous family and have initiated remarkable research interests over the past one decade in a large variety of fields. Herein, we have utilized a facile, one-step carbonization method to prepare fluorescent carbon dots using poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) both as a carbon source and as a surface passivating agent. The as prepared CDs emit bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet illumination. The structure and optical properties of the CDs are thoroughly investigated by several methods such as high-resolution transmission electron microscopy; dynamic light scattering; UV–vis, fluorescence and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. The CDs exhibit excellent water solubility and demonstrate average hydrodynamic diameter of 11.3nm, holding great promise for biological applications. The biocompatibility evaluation and in vitro imaging study reveals that the synthesized CDs can be used as effective fluorescent probes in bio-imaging without noticeable cytotoxicity. In addition, a unique sensor for the detection of vitamin B2 in aqueous solution is proposed on the basis of spontaneous fluorescence resonance energy transfer from CD to vitamin B2. These findings therefore suggest that the CDs can find potential applications in cellular imaging along with sensing of vitamin B2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Fluorescence
FRET
Sensor
Cell imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic illustration for the formation of CDs from PVA.

(a and b) TEM images of CDs with different resolution (inset a: size histogram of CDs, inset b: selected area FFT image). (c) High-resolution TEM image of CDs (scale bars=5nm). Lines and arrows indicate the lattice plane distances.
Size distribution of CDs obtained from DLS.
FTIR spectrum of CDs in KBr pellet.
UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectrum of synthesized CD [inset: photographs of CD dispersed in water under sunlight (left) and UV (365nm) illumination (right), respectively].
(a) Excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence spectra of CDs (from 270 to 430nm, with 10nm increment). (b) Normalized fluorescence spectra of CDs.
In vitro cytotoxicity of CDs against HeLa cells after 24 and 48h incubation at various final concentration of CD (0.1–1.0mg/mL); error bars represent three repetitive experiments.
Differential interference contrast (DIC) and fluorescence (F) images of HeLa cells in absence (a and b) and presence (c and d) of CDs.

Integral overlap between emission spectrum of CD (blue line) and absorption spectrum of RF (black line). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Fluorescence spectra of CD in the presence of different amounts of RF (inset: enlarged spectra showing fluorescence quenching of CD due to FRET process).
Calibration curve for the detection of vitamin B2.

head.ce:title: Facile and green approach to prepare fluorescent carbon dots: Emergent nanomaterial for cell imaging and detection of vitamin B2

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polymer Science Unit, Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science, Jadavpur, Kolkata 700 032, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Address: CAM, I.A.C.S., Jadavpur, Kolkata 700032, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a new representative in carbonaceous family and have initiated remarkable research interests over the past one decade in a large variety of fields. Herein, we have utilized a facile, one-step carbonization method to prepare fluorescent carbon dots using poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) both as a carbon source and as a surface passivating agent. The as prepared CDs emit bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet illumination. The structure and optical properties of the CDs are thoroughly investigated by several methods such as high-resolution transmission electron microscopy; dynamic light scattering; UV–vis, fluorescence and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. The CDs exhibit excellent water solubility and demonstrate average hydrodynamic diameter of 11.3nm, holding great promise for biological applications. The biocompatibility evaluation and in vitro imaging study reveals that the synthesized CDs can be used as effective fluorescent probes in bio-imaging without noticeable cytotoxicity. In addition, a unique sensor for the detection of vitamin B2 in aqueous solution is proposed on the basis of spontaneous fluorescence resonance energy transfer from CD to vitamin B2. These findings therefore suggest that the CDs can find potential applications in cellular imaging along with sensing of vitamin B2.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials have remarkable impact on the growth of a wide range of fields including electronics, photonics, energy, catalysis, medicine and so on. The discovery of semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) is considered to be a major breakthrough for the development of fluorescent nanomaterials. Semiconductor QDs usually possess strong and tunable fluorescence emission properties, high photostability and resistance to metabolic degradation which enable their applications in biosensing, bioimaging and biodiagnostics [1,2]. This prompted scientists of diverse fields to extensively investigate on semiconductor QDs for many years, although, these QDs contain heavy metals (Cd, Pb, Hg), whose well known toxicity and potential environmental hazards limit their further applications [3–5]. Hence, extensive efforts have been paid on the development of nontoxic and benign fluorescent nanomaterials as alternatives to the semiconductor QDs. In this regard, carbon nanomaterials such as nanodiamonds, carbon nanotubes, fullerene and graphene QDs [6–9] have appeared as promising candidates for numerous exciting applications, for example in bioimaging, biosensing, catalysis, and photovoltaic devices [4,10,11]. Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) or carbon dots (CDs), a novel class of heavy metal-free nanomaterials have emerged recently and have harvested much interest as potential competitors to conventional semiconductor QDs due to low toxicity, environmental friendliness, low cost and simple synthetic routes. Again, CDs render unique optical and physicochemical properties which encourage the development of novel imaging probes as well as biosensing platform for identifying challenging issues raised by biological research [12–14]. The aforementioned unique properties of CNPs/CDs have recently attracted considerable research interest to achieve wide range of applications. In the past few years, several techniques like laser-ablation methods [15], electrochemical synthesis [16], and microwave/ultrasonic synthesis [17,18] have been adopted to synthesize CDs. But, most of the above methods involve the use of expensive starting materials, strong acid, sophisticated instruments and complicated processes. Numerous carbon based materials like candle soot [19], bulk graphite [20], polymers [21–23], carbohydrates [24], and sugars [25] are used to produce CNPs/CDs. Again, in most cases the synthesized CDs generally require further surface passivation to improve their water solubility and fluorescence properties, which may limit their applications. Recently, CDs are enormously used for both cellular imaging and biosensing purposes [26–32]. For example, CDs produced from garlic is used for cell imaging of A549 cells by incubating with CDs [26] and the CDs produced from amino-terminated poly(ethylene glycol) is used for fluorescence imaging of stem cells [27]. N-doped CDs are utilized for the detection of catechol, dopamine, aflatoxin B1, etc. [28,29,32] whereas amino functionalized green fluorescent CD is being used as a biosensing platform to detect hyaluronidase [30].

It has been known that starch, cellulose, polylactide, poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA), etc. can be degraded using alkaline solution in presence of oxygen [33–36]. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is no report yet exist to produce CDs from the above polymers using alkaline medium. Here we chose PVA degradation in alkaline medium to study whether CDs can be produced in environment friendly condition. In this article, a facile and one-step hydrothermal route is designed to synthesize CDs from water soluble, biocompatible [37] polymer PVA without further surface modification. Here, PVA acts both as a carbon source and surface passivating agent due to the presence of hydroxyl groups. The resultant CDs display good dispersion in aqueous solution, strong blue fluorescence and low toxicity. These advantages indicate that the as synthesized CDs can be used as an excellent fluorescent probe for cellular imaging. In a recent article we have reported the sensitive and selective detection of Riboflavin (RF, commonly known as vitamin B2) using sulfonated graphene (SG), based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) from SG to RF at pH 4 [38]. But, the major disadvantage of this method is that we were unable to detect RF at biological pH due to low quantum yield of SG at this pH. To overcome this problem, in the present article, we have tactfully utilized our synthesized CDs as effective fluorescent sensing platform for RF owing to the spontaneous FRET process from CDs to RF at neutral pH. In addition, this method has high selectivity for detecting RF at pH=7, even in presence of other biologically relevant components such as nucleic acids (DNA, RNA), and protein (human serum albumin, HSA), amino acid (lysine). Conventional methods such as high performance liquid chromatography [39,40], capillary electrophoresis [41,42] and immunoassay [43] are used for the determination of RF. But the present approach for the detection of RF is proved to be superior to the above mentioned methods since FRET-based approach has many advantages including faster response, greater simplicity, lower cost and higher sensitivity and selectivity.

PVA (Mw
∼9000), Riboflavin, Calf thymus DNA (Type 1; sodium salt), RNA (diethyl amino ethanol salt, type IX from Torula Yeast), human serum albumin (HSA), lysine were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (USA). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was purchased from Merck (Mumbai). All the reagents were used as received.

For the synthesis of CDs, 500mg PVA was added to 20mL concentrated sodium hydroxide (1.25M) solution and stirred to get a homogenous solution. Then the solution was refluxed in a preheated oil bath at 120°C for 5h in air. After the completion of reflux process, the transparent viscous liquid turned into low viscosity liquid with pale yellow color. Then the resulting solution was cooled to room temperature and neutralized. The neutralized solution was dialyzed (MWCO=12kDa) against double distilled water and golden yellow colored solution was obtained which implies the formation of pure CDs. Finally, this solution was lyophilized to collect dry CDs for further use.

The synthesized CD was taken in double distilled water and it was sonicated for 15min, using an ultrasonic bath (60W, frequency 28kHz, Model AVIOC, Eyela) to make a homogenous dispersion (1mg/mL). DNA, RNA, HSA, lysine solutions (2mg/mL) were prepared by dissolving the required amount of them in water. For determination of RF, the concentration of CD was kept constant while the concentration of RF was varied from 0.13μM to 2.4μM.

The relative fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of CD was measured using quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (quantum yield 58% at 340nm) as a standard. The value of QY was calculated according to the following equation [44,45]:
QY
sample
=
QY
std .
[
( I / A )
sample
×
( A / I )
std .
]
η
sample
2
/
η
std .
2
where A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, η is the refractive index of solvent and I is the integrated emission intensity calculated from the area under the emission peak on the same wavelength scale. The absorbance values of both sample and standard solutions were kept below 0.1 to minimise the re-absorption effects at the excitation wavelength (340nm).

Human cervical cancer cells (HeLa cell) were seeded into 4-well chamber slides (cell seeding density: 2×104 per well) in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin/streptomycin at 37°C and 5% CO2 for overnight. For fluorescent imaging, CD solution was added to reach final concentration of 0.2mg/mL and incubated for 6h. After that, cells were washed with PBS buffer solution and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde solution. The fixed cells were mounted with 50% glycerol and fluorescence image was taken under UV light excitation.

The relative cytotoxicity of CD was evaluated by MTT [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide] assay. HeLa cells were seeded into 24-well tissue culture plates (cell seeding density: 5×104 per well) in DMEM medium supplemented with 10% heat-inactivated FBS and 1% penicillin/streptomycin with 5% CO2 at 37°C. After overnight growth, CD solution with different final concentration (0.1–1.0mg/mL) was added and incubated for 24h. After that, cells were washed with PBS buffer solution followed by addition of fresh supplemented DMEM medium (500μL) to each well. To observe the toxicity effect on cell morphology, optical microscope images of cells was captured. Then, for MTT assay 50μL of an aqueous solution of MTT (5mg/mL) was added to each well and incubated for 4h. The resultant violet formazan was dissolved in sodium dodecyl sulfate solution in water/DMF mixture. Absorbance of the solution was measured at 570nm using microplate reader. The relative cell viability was measured assuming 100% cell viability for cells without any CD. The percentage of cell viability was calculated by the following formula; cell viability%=(absorbance of treated sample/absorbance of untreated sample)×100.

UV–Vis absorption spectra of aqueous solutions of the samples were recorded with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Hewlett–Packard, model 8453) using a cuvette of 1cm path length. Fluorescence (excitation and emission) studies were performed in a Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluoromax 3 instrument using a cuvette of 1cm path length. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured by a time-correlated single photon counting fluoremeter (Fluorocube, Horiba Jobin Yvon) after excitation with a 340nm diode laser (nano LED of Horiba Jobin Yvon). The fluorescence images of the cell were captured by Carl Zeiss Apotome Imager Z1 and Olympus IX-81 fluorescence microscopes. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was studied on a Perkin Elmer Spectrum 100 FTIR spectrometer with solid KBr pellet. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential were measured using a NanoZS (Malvern) instrument. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) sample was prepared by drop casting diluted CD solution on a carbon-coated copper grid and observed in a UHR-FEG-TEM (JEOL, JEM 2100F) instrument at an accelerating voltage of 200kV.

The synthesis of blue light emitting CDs has been achieved by a simple NaOH assisted oxidation and thermal decomposition of PVA (Fig. 1 ) which does not require further surface modification steps. It is apparent from Fig. 1 that there is aerial oxidation on the secondary alcoholic OH group to CO group and finally to carboxylic acid [34,46]. Prolonged oxidation at high temperature cause the fragmentation of the polymer chains into small oxidized chains containing COOH, CO with some unreacted hydroxyl groups, finally leading to carbonization. The simultaneous oxidation and heating of the starting material in alkaline medium offers uniform nucleation of carbon particles and with time these particles grow though supramolecular organization producing the nano sized carbon clusters containing the above functional groups, and it is called as carbon dots. Thus PVA acts both as the carbon source and passivating agent which makes this synthetic procedure a fascinating one for preparation of CDs compared to other reported procedures [19–23]. This self passivated phenomenon furnishes high aqueous stability to the CDs and indeed the aqueous solutions of CDs remain stable for months.

The morphology and structures of CDs are confirmed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 2 a and b). The as synthesized CDs exhibit uniform dispersion and a relatively narrow size distribution ranging from 3 to 7nm with an average diameter of 4.5nm (Fig. 2a: inset). Again, the high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of CDs exhibit discernible lattice fringes with interplanar spacings of 0.28nm (Fig. 2c), which is close to the diffraction facet of graphitic carbon [47,48].

Dynamic light scattering (DLS) study has been performed to determine the hydrodynamic size of the synthesized CD. From the DLS measurements the average hydrodynamic diameter of the CD is calculated to be 11.3nm (Fig. 3 ). The difference in average diameter (as measured by TEM and DLS) may be attributed to the different states of the CDs under two measurement conditions. In case of TEM, the solvent (water) is removed, whereas for DLS, the sample remains swelled in the solvent, producing a larger diameter than that of TEM [49,50]. The surface charge of CD has been evaluated from zeta potential measurement. At pH 7.4 the synthesized CD shows negative surface charge (−11.3mV) due to the presence of carboxylate groups [51].

The FTIR spectrum characterizes the structural insights of the CDs as depicted in Fig. 4 . Besides the stretching vibrations of OH (3376cm−1), CH (2944cm−1) and deformation band of CH (1436cm−1) bonds in PVA (Fig. S1), the stretching vibration band of CO at 1720cm−1 combined with the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration bands of COC (1100–1400cm−1) indicates the existence of carboxylate groups on the CD surface. Thus FTIR data corroborates with the zeta potential value confirming the presence of carboxylate groups and the so formed CD is stabilized due to the self-passivation by PVA fragments.

The optical properties of the synthesized CDs are shown in Fig. 5 . The UV–vis absorption spectrum exhibits a broad peak around the range of 270–300nm which is ascribed to the typical absorption of n–π∗ transition of the carbonyl groups. The aqueous solution of CDs exhibits light yellow color (left inset, Fig. 5) in day light and bright blue emission under UV light (right inset in Fig. 5), which could be easily observed with the naked eye and has been taken with a digital camera. A broad emission centered at 441nm (blue 2 For interpretation of color in Fig. 5, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.
2 line, Fig. 5) is observed in the emission spectrum with an excitation wavelength at 350nm. The excitation spectrum (violet line, Fig. 5) reveals only one peak centered at 354nm, which signifies that the emission is related to one kind of transitions. Now, it is utmost necessary to mention here that the PVA solution itself is nonemissive in the visible region, confirming the bright blue fluorescence to be originating from the synthesized CDs. The excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence spectra of CD solution (1mg/mL) are shown in Fig. 6 a. As excitation wavelength increases from 270 to 430nm (with 10nm increment), the emission from CDs gradually shifted to higher wavelengths and the most intense emission is observed upon excitation at 360nm (Fig. 6a). Again, the red shift of emission spectra with increasing excitation wavelength is more clearly understood from the normalized fluorescence spectra depicted in Fig. 6b. No definite reason is known and it may be attributed to the different particle size and a considerable distribution of emissive trap sites on each CD [9,17]. This type of excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior is one of the most intriguing properties of CDs [47]. Thus in this case the origin of fluorescence property is assumed due to the presence of abundant surface defects (emissive traps), though several reasons are put forwarded [9,17,21,52]. The measured quantum yield of the synthesized CDs (8.5%, Table-S1) is higher than those of self-passivated CDs [17,53]. Fig. S2 represents the time resolved fluorescence decay profile of CDs and the average lifetime is calculated to be 3.42ns, which is very much close to those reported by others [17,53].

Though in the present CDs fluorescence quantum yield (8.5%) is lower than that of passivated CDs reported in literature [26,27], the very high longevity of the CDs (several months) make the system significantly attractive for application in biosensing process. The reported zeta potential is not good enough for colloidal stability but the present CDs exhibit water dispersibility which is envisaged from its invariant fluorescence intensity with time (Fig. S3). The stability of the dispersion may be attributed to the H-bonding interaction of the carboxylic and OH group of CDs with the water molecules.

Riboflavin (RF) is one of the most water-soluble vitamins that play key role in many physiological activities of cells such as nucleic acid repair process, cell apoptosis and electron transfer processes in the respiratory chain [54,55]. Vitamin B2 is also required for a wide variety of cellular processes, such as metabolism of fats, ketone bodies, carbohydrates and proteins, thus plays a crucial role in the human diet.

The integral overlap spectra of the absorbance spectrum of RF and emission spectrum of CD are shown in Fig. 9 . These spectra exhibit an obvious overlap between the emission spectrum of CD and absorption spectrum of RF indicating the possibility of a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process from the CDs (donor) to RF (acceptor). In order to detect RF in aqueous solution the fluorescence emission of CDs in presence of different amounts of RF are recorded and presented in Fig. 10 . From the spectra it is evident that the emission intensity of CD gradually decreases with significant blue shift of CD emission peak (430–409nm; Fig. 10, inset) whereas the intensity of RF in mixture solution increases with increase in concentration of RF. This phenomenon clearly demonstrates efficient FRET process between CD and RF. Here, the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs has strong attraction to CO and NH groups in RF to from hydrogen bonds [38], which decreases the spatial distance between CDs and RF that successfully assists CD-RF to act as an effective FRET pair. The FRET efficiency (E) has been determined from the steady-state measurement using the following equation [34,56]:
E = ( 1 -
F
DA
/
F
D
) × 100 %
where F is the donor fluorescence intensity in the absence (FD ) and presence (FDA ) of the acceptor. For 0.13μM addition of RF only 4% quenching takes place, whereas at 2.4μM RF concentration the quenching efficiency is ∼61%. Hence, with gradual addition of RF the FRET efficiency from CD to RF increases.

Now to get better insight of the FRET phenomenon, we have calculated the Förster distance using the following equation [44],
R
0
6
=
9000 ( ln 10 )
κ
2
φ
D
128
π
5
Nn
4
J ( λ )
where
φ
D
is the quantum yield of the donor in absence of acceptor, N is Avogadro’s number, n is the refractive index of the medium, J(λ) is the spectral overlap integral, which expresses the degree of spectral overlap between the donor emission and the acceptor absorption. The spectral overlap integral is defined as the following:
J ( λ ) =
∫
0
∞
F
D
( λ )
ε
A
( λ )
λ
4
d λ
where
F
D
( λ )
is the normalized emission spectrum of donor and it is dimensionless,
ε
A
( λ )
is the absorption coefficient of acceptor, and κ 2 is the orientation factor, which describes the relative orientation of the transition dipoles of the donor and acceptor in space. The value of κ 2 is usually assumed to be 2/3, which is appropriate for randomly oriented dipoles of donor and acceptor. If
ε
A
( λ )
is expressed in units of M−1 cm−1 and λ is in nanometres, then J(λ) is in units of M−1 cm−1 nm4. In our donor acceptor based system the spectral overlap integral is calculated to be 1.67×1024 M−1 cm−1 nm4. The calculated value of Förster distance (R 0) in our system is 5.064nm, facilitating efficient energy transfer between the donor–acceptor pair [44].

Again, we have detected RF in presence of biological mixtures containing DNA, RNA, HSA, and lysine. It is obvious from Fig. S5 that even in presence of different biomolecules FRET process is persistent, which corroborates that CD based system can be fruitful to selectively detect RF. The ratio of fluorescence intensity [F 0/F; where F 0 is the fluorescence intensity of donor (CD) in absence of the acceptor (RF) and F is that in the presence of acceptor RF] is plotted against the concentration of vitamin B2 and it has been fitted according to the equation F 0/F =0.8625+ K SV[RF], which is close to the Stern–Volmer equation (Fig. S6). From the above equation the bimolecular quenching constant (Kq ) is calculated to be 1.9×108 M−1 S−1.

The calibration curve for the detection of RF using FRET process (efficiency of FRET process vs. RF concentration) is presented in Fig. 11 . The calibration curve shows linearity in the described concentration range with correlation coefficient, R 2 =0.98081, which indicates that the present CD based optical sensor exhibit excellent promise to detect RF with high accuracy. Now, from the calibration curve the limit of detection (LOD) of vitamin B2 in CD based sensor has been calculated using the formula LOD=3×(SD)Blank/S, where SD is the standard deviation of background fluorescence (in absence of RF) and S is the slope of the calibration curve. The LOD of vitamin B2 in the present CD-RF system is calculated to be 1.2nM (Fig. 10), which, according to our best of knowledge, is the lowest among all previously reported detection methods [38–43].

In conclusion, we have successfully synthesized blue fluorescent carbon dots from PVA through a simple, green and economic approach. The synthesized CDs show excellent fluorescence properties, low toxicity as well as good water solubility. The CDs reveal an application in cell imaging with good fluorescence signal and high biocompatibility. Again, to realize their potential, the CDs are utilized as FRET based optical sensors to effectively detect biologically significant vitamin B2 with limit of detection (LOD) as low as 1.2nM. Moreover, the lower cytotoxicity of synthesized CD can be extended to detect vitamin B2 in vivo. Thus it is believed that these CDs could also offer great potential in other biotechnological applications and also in optoelectronic devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge DST New Delhi (Grant No SB/SI/OC-11/2013) for financial support. S. Nandi and P. Das acknowledge CSIR, New Delhi for providing the fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Table for quantum yield, FTIR spectrum of pure PVA, TCSPC plot, fluorescence stability of CDs, (DIC) images of HeLa cells in absence and presence of CDs, fluorescence spectra of CD-RF pair in presence of biomolecules and Stern-Volmer plot for quenching of RF. This material is available free of charge via the internet. Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2016.01.070.
